text:
Current state-of-the-art high-performance object detectors employ detection proposals to eliminate unnecessary search space via pruning, which substitutes the slow but exhaustive sliding window approach. Despite being included within a number of leading detection frameworks, it is still unclear what the underlying trade-offs are between different methods of proposal generation. In this work, we analyze 12 different proposal methods and 4 baselines along several metrics such as repeatability, as well as ground truth recall on PASCAL, ImageNet and MS COCO, to name a few, and finally the performance of the DPM, R-CNN and Fast R-CNN detection algorithms.

In this work we provide experimental evidence that both the precision of classification and the precision of proposal localization should be increased to improve object detection. In other words, it is as important to have precise proposals as it is to have high recall. We then define a new performance metric named Average Recall (AR) that takes into account both aspects. Our experimental results show that average recall correlates very highly with the performance of a detection algorithm and that it is better than any other evaluation metric using a single overlap threshold. Our goal in this work is to understand and expose the properties of proposal methods, providing a unifying perspective that will aid practitioners in choosing and fine-tuning proposal networks for diverse computer vision applications.
